1:
On Genesis 42:1 — “Now when Jacob saw that there was corn in Egypt, Jacob said unto his sons, Why do ye look one upon another?” — the witnesses mark the opening of the providential journey to Egypt.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the report. Ellicott: Jacob “saw,” that is, learned there was corn in Egypt, the report spreading by the caravan trade (Genesis 37:25). Wesley: he saw the corn his neighbours had bought and brought home. The Reformed Calvin: Moses begins to treat of the occasion that drew Jacob into Egypt, by hidden and unexpected methods of God’s providence.

The verse opens the famine narrative. Jacob, learning of grain in Egypt, rouses his irresolute sons. The thread of providence begins to draw the family toward Joseph.

So the witnesses see the hand of God begin, through famine, to draw Jacob’s house toward the long-lost Joseph. The witnesses note the report: Jacob “saw” — that is, came to learn or understand — that there was corn to be had in Egypt; as Ellicott observes, the news spread by the well-travelled caravan route between Canaan and Egypt, and Wesley adds that Jacob saw the corn his neighbours had already brought home. They mark the rebuke to his sons: “why do ye look one upon another?” — the brothers stand idle and irresolute in the face of the famine, gazing helplessly at one another while their father must rouse them to act. They observe Calvin’s opening point that Moses here begins the account of how God drew Jacob and his whole family into Egypt, and that we should mark by what hidden and unexpected methods God performs what he has decreed; the famine that threatens the family is in truth the means of its preservation and of the fulfilment of the dreams. They note that neither Jacob nor his sons can yet see where this journey will lead — to the throne of the brother they wronged. They observe that the ordinary pressures of life, hunger and need, are the instruments of a deeper purpose. The verse thus opens the great providential movement: driven by famine, Jacob bids his idle sons go down to Egypt — the first step, all unknowing, toward Joseph.
2:
On Genesis 42:2 — “And he said, Behold, I have heard that there is corn in Egypt: get you down thither, and buy for us from thence; that we may live, and not die” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s command to go down.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the narrative: Jacob bids his sons get down and buy, that they may live and not die; Joseph’s ten brethren went down. The Reformed Gill: this explains what is meant by Jacob’s “seeing.” Wesley: masters of families must not only pray for daily bread but with care and industry provide it.

The verse gives Jacob’s charge. Go down to Egypt and buy corn, that the family may live and not die. Necessity drives them toward Egypt.

So the witnesses see Jacob turn his knowledge of the corn into a definite command. The witnesses note that what was called his “seeing” in the previous verse is here explained as hearing: “I have heard that there is corn in Egypt” — the report has reached him, and he acts upon it. They mark the urgency of the charge: “get you down thither, and buy for us… that we may live, and not die” — the famine is severe, life itself is at stake, and the only remedy is the grain of Egypt; the father bestirs his hesitating sons to the journey that will keep the family alive. They observe Wesley’s practical lesson that the heads of families must not only pray for daily bread but, with care and industry, provide it; faith and diligence go together. They note the unconscious irony that the means of life for the whole family lies in the hand of the brother they sold for dead; in going down to buy bread, they go down to Joseph. They observe that the simple, pressing motive — that they may live and not die — is the lever by which God moves the whole house toward Egypt. They note that necessity, not design, drives them. The verse thus sets the family in motion: Jacob commands his sons to go down and buy corn, that they may live — sending ten of them, as the next verse records, on the road to Joseph.
3:
On Genesis 42:3 — “And Joseph’s ten brethren went down to buy corn in Egypt” — the witnesses mark the descent of the ten.

The Reformed Gill: they obeyed their father’s orders and immediately set out, ten of them going down together, all but Benjamin. The grammatical-historical Ellicott: either their households were already reduced by the famine, or each must have taken servants, if the corn carried home was to be of real use.

The verse records the journey. Joseph’s ten brethren go down to Egypt to buy corn. The very men who sold him travel to his domain.

So the witnesses see the ten brothers set out on the journey of obedience that will bring them, unknowing, to Joseph. The witnesses note their prompt compliance: they obeyed their father’s command and immediately went down; ten of them travelled together as one company. They mark the pointed designation: “Joseph’s ten brethren” — Moses names them not merely as Jacob’s sons but as Joseph’s brethren, recalling the very men who had conspired against him and sold him; now they go down to the land where he rules. They observe Ellicott’s practical note that, given the size of the family and the scale of the famine, either their households were already much reduced, or each brother must have taken servants and several beasts, so that enough corn could be carried home to be of real use. They note the providential irony that the brothers, in the simple errand of buying bread, are being led step by step to the brother whose dreams of their obeisance they had so resented. They observe that ten go down, but Benjamin is kept back — a detail the next verse explains. They note that the long-deferred meeting now draws near, though none of them suspects it. The verse thus sets the ten on the road: Joseph’s brethren go down to buy corn in Egypt — toward the reckoning the chapter unfolds.

So the witnesses see the ten brothers set out on the journey that will bring them, unknowing, to Joseph. They note their prompt compliance: they obeyed their father’s command and immediately went down, ten travelling together as one company. They mark the pointed designation “Joseph’s ten brethren” — Moses names them not merely as Jacob’s sons but as the very men who had conspired against Joseph and sold him; now they go down to the land where he rules. They observe Ellicott’s practical note that, given the family’s size and the famine, either their households were reduced or each took servants and beasts, that enough corn be carried home. They note the providential irony that the simple errand of buying bread is leading them step by step to the brother whose dreams they had resented.
4:
On Genesis 42:4 — “But Benjamin, Joseph’s brother, Jacob sent not with his brethren; for he said, Lest peradventure mischief befall him” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s protective fear for Benjamin.

The Reformed Gill notes that Benjamin is called “Joseph’s brother” because he was so by both father and mother, as the rest were not; Jacob kept him back, the youngest and his darling, the only son he had remaining (as he supposed) of his beloved Rachel.

The verse marks the exception. Jacob keeps Benjamin home, fearing harm. The father’s anxious love guards the last son of Rachel.

So the witnesses see Jacob single out Benjamin for protection, withholding him from the dangerous journey. The witnesses note the tender designation Moses uses: Benjamin is called “Joseph’s brother” — for, as Gill observes, he alone was Joseph’s full brother, by both father and mother, the two sons of the beloved Rachel, while the rest were brethren only by the father’s side. They mark the reason for keeping him back: “lest peradventure mischief befall him” — Jacob, having (as he believed) lost Joseph, clings the more anxiously to Benjamin, the youngest and now the only remaining son of Rachel, his darling. They observe the depth of the old man’s grief and fear: the loss of Joseph has made him guard the surviving brother with a jealous, trembling love, unwilling to risk him on the long and uncertain road to Egypt. They note the poignancy that Jacob’s fear of “mischief” befalling Benjamin foreshadows the very demand Joseph will make — that Benjamin be brought down. They observe that the father’s protective love, though natural and touching, will be sorely tried as the story unfolds. They note that the keeping back of Benjamin sets up the central tension of the coming chapters. The verse thus marks Jacob’s anxious exception: Benjamin, Rachel’s last son, is kept safe at home for fear of harm — the very son Joseph will require.

So the witnesses see Jacob single out Benjamin for protection, withholding him from the journey. They note the tender designation Moses uses: Benjamin is called “Joseph’s brother” — for, as Gill observes, he alone was Joseph’s full brother, by both father and mother, the two sons of the beloved Rachel, while the rest were brethren only by the father. They mark the reason for keeping him back: “lest peradventure mischief befall him” — Jacob, having (as he believed) lost Joseph, clings the more anxiously to Benjamin, the youngest and now the only remaining son of Rachel, his darling. They observe the poignancy that Jacob’s fear of “mischief” foreshadows the very demand Joseph will make — that Benjamin be brought down — setting up the central tension of the coming chapters.
5:
On Genesis 42:5 — “And the sons of Israel came to buy corn among those that came: for the famine was in the land of Canaan” — the witnesses mark the famine that reached even Canaan.

The Reformed Gill notes that the sons of Israel came among those that came to buy, whether the Egyptians or, particularly, the Canaanites and others, joining the throng of foreigners drawn to Egypt’s stores; for the famine was sore in Canaan as elsewhere.

The verse sets the brothers in the crowd. Israel’s sons come to buy among many, for the famine gripped Canaan too. The promised land itself is under the dearth.

So the witnesses see the sons of Jacob arrive in Egypt as part of a great throng driven by famine. The witnesses note the title given: “the sons of Israel” — the family of the patriarch to whom the promises were given, now reduced, like all others, to seeking bread in a foreign land. They mark that they came “among those that came” — not alone, but in the press of many foreigners, Canaanites and others, all drawn to Egypt because it alone had grain; Joseph’s wise storing had made Egypt the granary of the surrounding nations. They observe the reason stated: “for the famine was in the land of Canaan” — the dearth had reached even the land of promise, so that the heirs of the covenant were not exempt from the common distress; the same providence that afflicted the nations afflicted them, and drove them to the same refuge. They note the sobering truth that the people of God share in the trials and hungers of the world, and must seek relief by the same means; the promise did not spare Jacob’s house from famine, but used the famine to lead them. They observe that, lost in the crowd of buyers, the brothers little knew that the governor who fed the nations was their own brother. The verse thus sets them in the multitude: the sons of Israel come to buy among many, driven by the famine that gripped even Canaan — into the presence, the next verse shows, of Joseph the governor.
6:
On Genesis 42:6 — “And Joseph was the governor over the land… and Joseph’s brethren came, and bowed down themselves before him with their faces to the earth” — the witnesses mark the fulfilment of the dreams in the brothers’ obeisance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the office. Adam Clarke: “governor” (shallit), a protector, one set over the land; whence the later title Sultan. Ellicott: Joseph sold by deputy throughout Egypt, but so large a party drew his notice. The Reformed Calvin: Moses connects Joseph’s honour with his fidelity and diligence.

The verse fulfils the dreams. Joseph the governor receives his brothers, who bow before him to the earth. The boy’s vision of their obeisance comes to pass.

So the witnesses see the long-derided dreams of Joseph fulfilled before his face. The witnesses note his station: “Joseph was the governor over the land” — the supreme authority under Pharaoh, especially over the corn and its sale; the word, as Clarke observes, denotes a ruler and protector set over the land, the very title (in its Arabic form) of the later Sultans. They mark Calvin’s point that Moses joins Joseph’s high honour to his fidelity and diligence: he was raised because he was faithful. They observe the great moment: “Joseph’s brethren came, and bowed down themselves before him with their faces to the earth” — the very men who had scorned his dreams of their sheaves and stars bowing to his, now prostrate themselves before him in lowliest homage, all unknowing. They note the exact fulfilment of the boyhood visions (Genesis 37:7, Genesis 37:9): what they had hated him for foretelling, and had tried to thwart by selling him, their own act in coming to buy corn now brings to pass; God overruled their malice to accomplish the very thing they resisted. They observe Ellicott’s note that, though Joseph ordinarily sold by deputy, so large a party as the ten brethren would naturally come before the governor himself. They note the irony that they fulfil the dreams in the act of seeking to escape death. The verse thus stages the fulfilment: the brothers bow to the earth before Joseph the governor — the dreams accomplished, as he, the next verse shows, knows them at once.
7:
On Genesis 42:7 — “And Joseph saw his brethren, and he knew them, but made himself strange unto them, and spake roughly unto them” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s feigned harshness.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: Joseph has been accused of harshness, but his aim was higher; he longed to see Benjamin and to prove his brethren. The Puritan Henry and Wesley wonder that Joseph never sent word to his father in twenty years. The Reformed Calvin: he was not impelled by revenge, but by a higher design.

The verse begins the testing. Joseph knows his brothers but feigns to be a stranger and speaks roughly. His harshness masks a loving purpose.

So the witnesses see Joseph, recognizing his brothers, deliberately mask himself and speak harshly — the beginning of his testing of them. The witnesses note that he “knew them” at once, though they did not know him; yet he “made himself strange” — assumed the manner of a foreign stranger — and “spake roughly,” treating them with severity. They mark the question the expositors raise about this seeming cruelty, and their answer: Joseph is not acting from revenge or bitterness. Calvin insists he was impelled not by a desire to retaliate but by a higher and worthier design; Ellicott likewise defends him, finding his aim not in harshness but in his longing to see Benjamin and, above all, to prove and bring his brothers to repentance. They observe that the rough speech is a disguise of love: he must discover whether they are changed men, and the testing requires that he conceal his heart for a time. They note the puzzle Henry and Wesley raise — why Joseph, in twenty years of power, never sent word to his grieving father; the expositors suppose he waited on God’s guidance, or for the very meeting now unfolding, to bring the whole family together. They observe that the harshness is measured and purposeful, not vindictive; beneath it the brother yearns. The verse thus opens the long testing: Joseph knows his brethren, but feigns the stranger and speaks roughly — a severity that serves, as the chapter shows, a gracious end.
8:
On Genesis 42:8 — “And Joseph knew his brethren, but they knew not him” — the witnesses mark the one-sided recognition.

The Reformed Gill explains why they failed to know him: some twenty-two years had passed since they saw him, then a beardless youth, now a bearded prince, clothed as an Egyptian, speaking the Egyptian tongue, in the highest station. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott trace the repeated “knew,” the brothers’ ignorance setting off Joseph’s recognition.

The verse states the contrast. Joseph knew his brethren; they knew not him. The disguise of years and station hides him from them.

So the witnesses see the poignant imbalance: Joseph knows them, but they have no notion who he is. The witnesses note the reasons the expositors give for the brothers’ failure to recognize him. Some twenty-two years had passed since they last saw him; the youth they sold was beardless, but now a full-grown, bearded man stood before them. He was clothed in the rich garb of an Egyptian prince, not the coat of a Hebrew shepherd-boy. He spoke through an interpreter in the Egyptian tongue, not in their own Hebrew. And he held the highest office in the land, the last place they would look for their lost brother — whom they supposed long dead or enslaved. They mark that every outward mark by which they might have known him was altered or concealed, while to Joseph their faces, though aged, were unmistakable. They observe the deep pathos of the scene: the brother gazes on the men who wronged him, knowing all, while they stand blind before the very one they sold, fulfilling his dreams without suspecting it. They note that this one-sided knowledge gives Joseph the power and the freedom to test them as he will. They observe that the disguise is providential, allowing the long, searching proof of their hearts to proceed. The verse thus fixes the contrast: Joseph knew his brethren, but they knew not him — and the sight of them, as the next verse shows, brings his old dreams to mind.

So the witnesses see the poignant imbalance: Joseph knows them, but they have no notion who he is. They note the reasons the expositors give for the brothers’ failure: some twenty-two years had passed, the beardless youth they sold now a full-grown, bearded man; he was clothed as an Egyptian prince, not a Hebrew shepherd-boy; he spoke through an interpreter in Egyptian, not their Hebrew; and he held the highest office, the last place they would look for their lost, supposedly dead brother. They mark that every outward mark by which they might have known him was altered or concealed, while their faces were unmistakable to him. They observe the deep pathos: the brother gazes on the men who wronged him, knowing all, while they stand blind before the very one they sold. They note that this one-sided knowledge gives Joseph the freedom to test them as he will.
9:
On Genesis 42:9 — “And Joseph remembered the dreams which he dreamed of them, and said unto them, Ye are spies; to see the nakedness of the land ye are come” — the witnesses mark the remembered dreams and the charge of spying.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both. Adam Clarke: “spies” (meraggelim), those who scout out a land’s defences. Ellicott: every traveller in the East fell under such suspicion, the Semitic race especially distrusted in Egypt. Wesley: he remembered the dreams, but they had forgot them; the laying up of God’s oracles in the heart guides our conduct.

The verse joins memory and accusation. Joseph recalls his dreams as they bow, and charges them as spies. The dreams direct his dealings with them.

So the witnesses see Joseph, the dreams fulfilled before him, begin the testing with a charge of espionage. The witnesses note the memory awakened: “Joseph remembered the dreams which he dreamed of them” — their bowing before him brought vividly to mind the boyhood visions of their sheaves and the sun, moon, and stars doing him obeisance; what was once a cause of their hatred is now accomplished. They mark Wesley’s fine observation that Joseph remembered the dreams, but they had forgotten them; and that the laying up of God’s oracles in the heart is of excellent use to guide our conduct — Joseph, eyeing his divine dreams, shapes his dealings with his brethren toward their fulfilment and their good. They observe the accusation: “ye are spies; to see the nakedness of the land ye are come” — he charges them with coming to scout out the undefended parts, the “nakedness,” of Egypt; the word, Clarke notes, means those who survey a land’s weaknesses for hostile ends. They note Ellicott’s point that such suspicion fell on every traveller in the East, and the Semitic peoples especially were watched with distrust in Egypt, where frontier fortresses guarded against incursion; the charge was thus plausible. They observe that the accusation is a deliberate device to test and probe his brethren, not a sincere belief. The verse thus opens the proving: remembering his dreams, Joseph charges his brothers as spies — a pretext to search their hearts and draw out the truth they now begin to tell.
10:
On Genesis 42:10 — “And they said unto him, Nay, my lord, but to buy food are thy servants come” — the witnesses mark the brothers’ humble denial.

The Reformed Gill notes their reverent reply: one in the name of all, or each in turn, denying the charge and addressing him with the greatest submission, calling him “my lord” and themselves his “servants” — thus further fulfilling Joseph’s dreams. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the exchange.

The verse gives their reply. They protest they come only to buy food, not to spy. Their submissive words deepen the dreams’ fulfilment.

So the witnesses see the brothers answer the charge with earnest, humble denial. The witnesses note the form of their reply: “Nay, my lord” — spoken, as Gill suggests, by one in the name of all, or by each in turn; a flat denial of the accusation of spying. They mark the deference of their address: they call him “my lord” and style themselves “thy servants” — the very language of subjection, bowing in word as they had bowed in body. They observe the unconscious irony the expositors point out: in thus humbling themselves before Joseph, calling him lord and themselves his servants, they continue to fulfil the dreams they once derided — the dreams in which his sheaf stood upright and theirs bowed down. They note the plain substance of their defence: “to buy food are thy servants come” — their errand is honest and ordinary, the simple purchase of grain in a time of famine, with no design against the land. They observe that the more they protest and explain themselves, the more they reveal, drawing out the family particulars Joseph seeks. They note the genuine alarm beneath their courtesy: accused before so great a ruler, they press their innocence with all submission. The verse thus records their humble denial: they come only to buy food, not to spy — a protest they enlarge, in the next verse, into a plea of their honest family origin.

So the witnesses see the brothers answer the charge with earnest, humble denial. They note the form of their reply: “Nay, my lord” — spoken, as Gill suggests, by one in the name of all, or each in turn; a flat denial of the accusation. They mark the deference of their address: they call him “my lord” and style themselves “thy servants” — the language of subjection, bowing in word as they had bowed in body. They observe the unconscious irony: in thus humbling themselves before Joseph, they continue to fulfil the dreams they once derided. They note the plain substance of their defence: “to buy food are thy servants come” — an honest, ordinary errand in famine, with no design against the land; yet the more they protest and explain, the more they reveal, drawing out the family particulars Joseph seeks.
11:
On Genesis 42:11 — “We are all one man’s sons; we are true men, thy servants are no spies” — the witnesses mark the plea of common parentage.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the argument. Adam Clarke: they belong to one family, not different tribes; it is not likely one family would attempt a kingdom. Ellicott: a sound plea, for no man would send his whole family on so dangerous an errand; they press their family relations as proof.

The verse gives their defence. Being all one man’s sons and honest men, they could be no spies. Family unity argues their innocence.

So the witnesses see the brothers plead their common parentage as proof of their honesty. The witnesses note the argument: “we are all one man’s sons” — they are not a band gathered from different tribes or nations for some hostile purpose, but the sons of a single father, one family; and, as Clarke and Ellicott observe, it is wholly unlikely that one man would send all his sons together as spies on so perilous an errand, risking his whole house at once. They mark the soundness of the reasoning the expositors commend: a father bent on espionage would not stake his entire family; their travelling together as brothers is itself an argument for an innocent, domestic purpose. They observe their direct assertion: “we are true men” — honest, upright men of integrity; “thy servants are no spies” — flatly repudiating the charge. They note that, pressed by the accusation, they offer their family bond as their credential, unwittingly disclosing the very particulars — their number, their father, their brothers — that Joseph wishes to learn. They observe the providence at work: the testing draws from them the account of the family, leading toward the mention of Benjamin and the absent one. They note their earnestness, the plea of honest men cornered by a grave charge. The verse thus presses their defence: as one man’s sons and true men they are no spies — an argument Joseph, in the next verse, refuses to accept.

So the witnesses see the brothers plead their common parentage as proof of their honesty. They note the argument: “we are all one man’s sons” — not a band gathered from different tribes for some hostile purpose, but the sons of a single father; and, as Clarke and Ellicott observe, it is unlikely one man would send all his sons as spies on so perilous an errand, risking his whole house at once. They mark the soundness the expositors commend: their travelling together as brothers is itself an argument for an innocent purpose. They observe their direct assertion: “we are true men… thy servants are no spies” — flatly repudiating the charge. They note that, pressed by the accusation, they offer their family bond as their credential, unwittingly disclosing the very particulars — their number, their father, their brothers — that Joseph wishes to learn.
12:
On Genesis 42:12 — “And he said unto them, Nay, but to see the nakedness of the land ye are come” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s persistence in the charge.

The Reformed Gill notes the force of his reply: this argument will not satisfy him; if they can produce no better proof of being honest men, or give no better account of themselves, he must abide by his charge that they have come to spy out the land’s undefended parts.

The verse repeats the accusation. Joseph rejects their plea and presses the charge of spying. He drives them to disclose more of themselves.

So the witnesses see Joseph refuse to be satisfied, pressing the accusation a second time. The witnesses note his blunt rejection of their plea: “Nay” — he will not accept their bare assertion of honesty; the family argument, however sound, is brushed aside. They mark his renewed charge: “but to see the nakedness of the land ye are come” — he insists again that they have come to scout out the weak and undefended places of Egypt for hostile ends. They observe the purpose Gill discerns in this persistence: Joseph means to drive them to give a fuller account of themselves; if they can offer no better proof than their word, he will hold them to the charge — and so compel them to disclose more of their family and circumstances. They note that the repeated accusation is a deliberate pressure, forcing the brothers to say more than they otherwise would, and leading them, in the next verse, to reveal the existence of the youngest brother and the one who “is not.” They observe that Joseph’s severity here is calculated, not capricious: each rebuff is a step toward drawing out the truth he seeks about Benjamin and his father. They note that the testing advances by his refusal to be easily convinced. The verse thus continues the proving: Joseph rejects their defence and repeats the charge — pressing them, in the next verse, to tell of the brethren left behind.

So the witnesses see Joseph refuse to be satisfied, pressing the accusation a second time. They note his blunt rejection of their plea: “Nay” — he will not accept their bare assertion of honesty; the family argument, however sound, is brushed aside. They mark his renewed charge: “but to see the nakedness of the land ye are come” — he insists again that they have come to scout out the weak and undefended places of Egypt. They observe the purpose Gill discerns: Joseph means to drive them to a fuller account of themselves; if they can offer no better proof than their word, he will hold them to the charge, and so compel them to disclose more of their family. They note that the repeated accusation is deliberate pressure, leading them, in the next verse, to reveal the youngest brother and the one who “is not”; the severity is calculated, each rebuff a step toward the truth about Benjamin.
13:
On Genesis 42:13 — “Thy servants are twelve brethren, the sons of one man in the land of Canaan; and, behold, the youngest is this day with our father, and one is not” — the witnesses mark the disclosure of the family.

The Reformed Gill notes they say they “are” (or rather were) twelve brethren, since one is afterwards said not to be. The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “one is not” is an elliptical phrase — one is not alive.

The verse discloses the whole family. They name twelve brethren, the youngest at home, and one who is not. The pressure draws out the truth of Joseph and Benjamin.

So the witnesses see the brothers, pressed by the charge, lay open the whole account of their family. The witnesses note the number: “thy servants are twelve brethren, the sons of one man in the land of Canaan” — they widen their earlier plea, naming the full count of the brothers and their single father and homeland, as the credential of their honesty. They mark the two who are absent: “the youngest is this day with our father, and one is not.” They observe Clarke’s note that “one is not” is an elliptical expression meaning one is no longer alive — they suppose Joseph dead. They note Gill’s point on the present tense: they say they “are” twelve, though in truth one was thought gone; yet, unknown to them, all twelve still lived, the supposed-dead brother standing before them as their judge. They observe the deep, unintended significance of their words: in mentioning the youngest at home (Benjamin) and the one who “is not” (Joseph himself), they hand Joseph exactly the knowledge he sought — that his full brother lives and that his father survives. They note the pathos that they speak of Joseph as one dead while looking upon him, and that the casual phrase “one is not” carries the weight of their old crime. They observe that the testing has now drawn out the whole truth of the family. The verse thus yields the disclosure: twelve brethren, the youngest at home, and one who “is not” — the words that, in the next verse, Joseph turns back upon them.
14:
On Genesis 42:14 — “And Joseph said unto them, That is it that I spake unto you, saying, Ye are spies” — the witnesses mark Joseph turning their words against them.

The Reformed Gill notes that Joseph treats their account as confirming his suspicion. The grammatical-historical Ellicott: Joseph persists in the charge because, besides the information gained, he wished to get Benjamin into his power, to have him with him; as for the rest, he had as yet no settled purpose.

The verse turns their disclosure back. Joseph treats their own account as proof of spying. He uses the charge to reach Benjamin.

So the witnesses see Joseph seize on the brothers’ own words as fresh ground for his charge. The witnesses note his rejoinder: “that is it that I spake unto you, saying, Ye are spies” — he treats their account of themselves, far from clearing them, as confirming his suspicion; their very explanation, he claims, proves his point. They mark that this is, of course, a pretext: the information they have just given him — that the youngest is at home and one is gone — is precisely what he wanted, and he uses the charge not because he believes it but to press them further. They observe Ellicott’s discernment of his real aim: Joseph persists in the accusation chiefly because he wishes to get Benjamin into his power, to have his full brother brought down to him; the demand to produce the youngest will be his lever. They note Ellicott’s further point that, as for the rest of his brethren, Joseph had as yet no settled purpose; his plan was unfolding step by step, shaped by his longing for Benjamin and his desire to prove the others. They observe that the testing thus advances by Joseph’s turning their disclosure into a renewed demand. They note that beneath the maintained severity, the loving design — to see Benjamin and to reach his father — is at work. The verse thus shows Joseph pressing his advantage: their own words become the warrant for his charge — the charge by which, in the next verse, he demands the youngest brother be produced.
15:
On Genesis 42:15 — “Hereby ye shall be proved: By the life of Pharaoh ye shall not go forth hence, except your youngest brother come hither” — the witnesses mark the test and the oath.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the oath. Adam Clarke: “By the life of Pharaoh” (chey Pharaoh) — as surely as Pharaoh lives; it is the oath they themselves make it. Ellicott: swearing by the king’s life was common in ancient times (1 Samuel 17:55). The Reformed Calvin raises the question of swearing.

The verse sets the test. They shall be proved by producing the youngest, on the oath of Pharaoh’s life. The demand fixes on Benjamin.

So the witnesses see Joseph set the terms of the proof, fastening on the youngest brother. The witnesses note the condition: “hereby ye shall be proved” — the test of their honesty is to be the producing of Benjamin; if they are true men, let them bring down the brother they have named. They mark that the demand falls precisely where Joseph’s heart is set — on his own full brother — while wearing the guise of a proof of their veracity. They observe the oath: “by the life of Pharaoh ye shall not go forth hence, except your youngest brother come hither” — Joseph binds the requirement with a solemn asseveration. They note the expositors’ remarks on this form: Ellicott observes that swearing by the king’s life was an ancient custom; Clarke renders it “as surely as Pharaoh lives,” and notes it is the brothers who reckon it an oath; Calvin raises the question whether Joseph did well to swear so, since the godly were taught to swear only by the name of God. They observe that Joseph speaks here in his assumed character as an Egyptian official, using the language of the court. They note the firmness of the demand: they shall not leave Egypt until Benjamin is brought; the meeting with the youngest is made the price of their freedom. They observe that the test is the means by which Joseph will bring his father’s darling, and so the whole family, down to him. The verse thus fixes the proof: they shall be tried by producing the youngest, on the oath of Pharaoh’s life — a demand Joseph qualifies, in the next verse, by sending one to fetch him.
16:
On Genesis 42:16 — “Send one of you, and let him fetch your brother, and ye shall be kept in prison… whether there be any truth in you: or else by the life of Pharaoh surely ye are spies” — the witnesses mark the proposed proof.

The Reformed Gill notes the proposal: one of them is to be sent to fetch Benjamin from their father’s house, while the rest are held as pledges, that the truth of their words might be tested.

The verse details the test. One shall fetch Benjamin while the rest are held, to prove their words. Their honesty is staked on the youngest’s coming.

So the witnesses see Joseph lay out the mechanism of the proof. The witnesses note the proposal: “send one of you, and let him fetch your brother” — a single brother is to be dispatched to Canaan to bring Benjamin down, while “ye shall be kept in prison” — the rest detained as hostages, securing the messenger’s return and the truth of their account. They mark the stated purpose: “that your words may be proved, whether there be any truth in you” — the producing of the youngest is made the decisive test of their veracity; if Benjamin comes, they are shown to be honest men; if not, their tale is exposed as false. They observe the renewed oath and threat: “or else by the life of Pharaoh surely ye are spies” — should they fail the test, the charge of espionage stands confirmed against them. They note that the whole device is aimed, beneath its severity, at one end: to bring Benjamin to Egypt and into Joseph’s sight. They observe that Gill rightly reads the proposal as a means of testing the truth of their words by the demand for the youngest. They note the pressure this places on the brothers, faced with prison and the dreaded necessity of telling their father he must give up Benjamin. They observe that the plan, as first proposed, will be softened in the verses following. The verse thus sets the terms of the proof: one to fetch Benjamin, the rest held in pledge, that their words be tried — a sentence Joseph enforces, in the next verse, by three days’ confinement.
17:
On Genesis 42:17 — “And he put them all together into ward three days” — the witnesses mark the three days’ confinement.

The Reformed Gill suggests the three days were given them to consult and agree who should be sent to fetch Benjamin — a matter, it seems, on which none of them could readily agree, none caring to be the bringer of such evil tidings to their father. Calvin: by the act itself Joseph shows himself severe, shutting them all up, tormenting them with fear.

The verse records the imprisonment. Joseph holds them all in ward three days. The severity presses on their consciences.

So the witnesses see Joseph carry his severity from words to deeds, shutting all his brethren up together for three days. The witnesses note Calvin’s observation that here, not merely by words as before but by the act itself, Joseph shows himself stern toward his brethren, confining them all as if about to bring them to punishment, and for three days tormenting them with fear. They mark Gill’s suggestion as to the purpose this interval served the brothers: it gave them time to consult and to decide which of them should be sent home to fetch Benjamin — a question, it appears, that none of them could readily settle, since none was willing to be the one to carry to their father the dreadful news that the youngest must be given up. They observe that the confinement was a real affliction, calculated to humble them and to work upon their consciences; shut up in a strange land, accused, and helpless, they are brought low. They note that this pressure prepares the way for the penitent reflection that breaks from them once Joseph relents and changes his demand; the imprisonment softens and searches their hearts. They observe that the severity, though feigned in its pretext, serves a genuine end: to bring the brothers to remember and confess their old sin. They note that Joseph holds them just long enough to make them feel their plight. The verse thus records the three days’ ward — a confinement after which, the next verse shows, Joseph relents and offers a milder term.
18:
On Genesis 42:18 — “And Joseph said unto them the third day, This do, and live; for I fear God” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s relenting and his fear of God.

The Reformed Gill: his heart yearning toward them, finding they could not agree who should go, he recedes from his former order. The grammatical-historical commentators note the divine name. Adam Clarke: “I fear God” (eth haelohim). Ellicott: by using the name Elohim they would understand he worshipped the same God as they.

The verse shows Joseph relenting. On the third day he offers a milder term, for he fears God. His severity is governed by piety.

So the witnesses see Joseph, on the third day, soften his demand and reveal the principle of his dealing: the fear of God. The witnesses note the relenting: “Joseph said unto them the third day” — his heart, as Gill discerns, yearning toward his brethren, and finding they could not agree who should go, he draws back from his first harsh order and proposes a gentler one. They mark the offer: “This do, and live” — he holds out the prospect of life and release on milder terms than the imprisonment of all but one. They observe the striking ground he gives: “for I fear God” — Joseph professes that his conduct is governed not by caprice or cruelty but by the fear of God, who will not let him deal unjustly with helpless men. They note Ellicott’s point that by using the name Elohim he would have his brethren understand that he worshipped the same God as they; the rough Egyptian governor confesses the true God, a hint, all unrecognized, of who he is. They observe Clarke’s attention to the emphatic phrase, marking Joseph’s reverence. They note that the fear of God is the restraint upon his severity: it keeps his testing within the bounds of mercy and justice, and assures the brethren they may trust him. They observe that this self-disclosure of piety both reassures them and quietly reveals his true character beneath the disguise. The verse thus shows Joseph relenting in the fear of God: offering life on milder terms — the gentler proposal he details in the next verses.
19:
On Genesis 42:19 — “If ye be true men, let one of your brethren be bound in the house of your prison: go ye, carry corn for the famine of your houses” — the witnesses mark the softened terms.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the new proposal: instead of holding all but one, Joseph will hold but one and let the rest go with corn for their starving households, on condition the youngest be brought. The Reformed Gill: if they are honest, as they claim, let this be the test.

The verse reverses the first demand. Now one shall be held and the rest go with corn. Mercy tempers the test for the famished family.

So the witnesses see Joseph reverse his harsher first proposal, holding only one and freeing the rest. The witnesses note the changed condition: “if ye be true men, let one of your brethren be bound in the house of your prison” — where he had first proposed to keep them all and send one home, now he will detain only a single brother as hostage and release the others. They mark the mercy in the new terms: “go ye, carry corn for the famine of your houses” — the nine are to return at once with grain, that their starving households in Canaan not perish; Joseph will not let the famine slay the wider family while he tests the brothers. They observe that the test still stands — they must prove their honesty — but it is now framed so as to relieve the famished rather than to risk them; one is held, but bread goes home. They note the consideration this shows for the innocent women and children of the family, dependent on the corn; Joseph’s severity toward the guilty brothers does not extend to the helpless who hunger. They observe that the “if ye be true men” keeps the condition before them: the proof of their veracity is still the bringing of Benjamin. They note that the softened terms reveal again the fear of God and the yearning heart that govern Joseph’s dealing. The verse thus eases the test: one held, the rest sent home with corn — on the condition, stated in the next verse, that the youngest be brought.
20:
On Genesis 42:20 — “But bring your youngest brother unto me; so shall your words be verified, and ye shall not die. And they did so” — the witnesses mark the condition and the brothers’ submission.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: besides his longing to see his brother, Joseph felt that having Benjamin would be the best means of bringing his father also to Egypt; he substitutes a milder proposal for his former one. Keil and Delitzsch trace the agreement.

The verse states the condition. Bring the youngest, and their words are verified and their lives spared. They submit to the terms.

So the witnesses see Joseph fix the condition of the test and the brothers accept it. The witnesses note the demand at its heart: “bring your youngest brother unto me” — the producing of Benjamin remains the decisive proof; on this all turns. They mark the promise attached: “so shall your words be verified, and ye shall not die” — if they bring the youngest, their claim to be honest men will be confirmed and their lives secured; the test, though severe, holds out life. They observe Ellicott’s discernment of Joseph’s twofold motive: besides his deep longing to see his only full brother again, Joseph reckons that the possession of Benjamin will be the surest means of drawing his aged father, too, down to Egypt; the demand for the youngest is a step toward gathering the whole family. They note that the milder proposal substituted for the first is calculated to achieve this end without crushing the family. They observe the brothers’ response: “and they did so” — they accept the terms, submitting to leave one behind and to undertake the dreaded errand of bringing Benjamin. They note that this submission, made under pressure and fear, sets the stage for the penitent reckoning that immediately follows. They observe that the condition draws the family inexorably toward reunion. The verse thus seals the terms: bring the youngest, and live — to which the brothers consent, falling, in the next verse, into the remembrance of their guilt.

So the witnesses see Joseph fix the condition of the test and the brothers accept it. They note the demand at its heart: “bring your youngest brother unto me” — the producing of Benjamin remains the decisive proof; on this all turns. They mark the promise attached: “so shall your words be verified, and ye shall not die” — the test, though severe, holds out life. They observe Ellicott’s discernment of Joseph’s twofold motive: besides his longing to see his only full brother, he reckons that possessing Benjamin will be the surest means of drawing his aged father down to Egypt too; the demand is a step toward gathering the whole family. They note the brothers’ response: “and they did so” — they accept the terms, submitting to leave one behind and undertake the dreaded errand — a submission that sets the stage for the penitent reckoning that immediately follows.
21:
On Genesis 42:21 — “And they said one to another, We are verily guilty concerning our brother, in that we saw the anguish of his soul, when he besought us, and we would not hear; therefore is this distress come upon us” — the witnesses mark the awakening of conscience.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: how finely the office of conscience is shown! About twenty-two years had passed; God combines the circumstances to awaken it. The Puritan Henry: their penitent reflection on the wrong done to Joseph. The Reformed Calvin: a remarkable passage — reduced to straits, they recall the fratricide of years before. Wesley: now the matter came to an issue, they began to relent.

The verse records their remorse. The brothers own their guilt for Joseph’s anguish, and read their distress as its requital. Conscience, long silent, awakes.

So the witnesses see the brothers, under affliction, broken into the confession of a crime buried for twenty years. The witnesses note the awakening of conscience that Clarke so admires: after some twenty-two years of silence, God so combines the present circumstances — the accusation, the prison, the helplessness — that the long-dulled conscience is roused at last; their sin against Joseph rises up before them. They mark Calvin’s observation that before affliction pressed upon them they lay in a kind of torpor, but now, reduced to straits, they recall the fratricidal cruelty committed thirteen and more years before; trouble is the means God uses to bring sin to remembrance. They observe the substance of their confession: “we are verily guilty concerning our brother” — they own their guilt; and they recall the very scene of it: “in that we saw the anguish of his soul, when he besought us, and we would not hear” — the memory of the young Joseph pleading for his life from the pit, and their pitiless refusal, now returns to accuse them. They note the just inference they draw: “therefore is this distress come upon us” — they read their present trouble as the righteous requital of that old, unrepented sin; conscience reads providence. They observe Wesley’s point that now, the matter coming to an issue, they began to relent. They note that this is true penitence beginning to stir — the very fruit Joseph’s testing sought. The verse thus records the awakening: the brothers confess their guilt for Joseph’s anguish and own their distress as its just return — words Joseph hears, in the next verses, unsuspected.
22:
On Genesis 42:22 — “And Reuben answered them, saying, Spake I not unto you… Do not sin against the child; and ye would not hear? therefore, behold, also his blood is required” — the witnesses mark Reuben’s reproach.

The Reformed Calvin: because Reuben had tried to deliver Joseph, he magnifies their fault in not listening to prudent counsel, conveying a reproof. The grammatical-historical Ellicott: they evidently thought Joseph dead, so the charge of falsehood for saying “one is not” is groundless; “his blood is required” expresses their dread of bloodguilt.

The verse adds Reuben’s rebuke. He recalls his warning against harming the child, now unheeded, and dreads the reckoning for Joseph’s blood. The eldest’s words sharpen their remorse.

So the witnesses see Reuben, the eldest, deepen the brothers’ self-accusation with the bitter “I told you so.” The witnesses note his appeal to his own past counsel: “Spake I not unto you, saying, Do not sin against the child; and ye would not hear?” — Reuben, who had once tried to save Joseph from the pit and restore him to their father, now recalls that warning, which they had refused; Calvin observes that he magnifies their fault precisely because they would not listen to his prudent counsel at the time, and his words carry a reproof. They mark the dread he voices: “therefore, behold, also his blood is required” — he reads their present trouble as the demanded reckoning for Joseph’s blood, the guilt of which now falls upon them; the fear of bloodguilt presses on the conscience. They observe Ellicott’s note that the brothers plainly believed Joseph dead, so that their earlier saying “one is not” was no deliberate lie; and that “his blood is required” expresses their terror that they must answer for having, as they supposed, destroyed him. They note that Reuben’s reproach, far from comforting, sharpens the general remorse, fixing the blame and naming the dreaded penalty. They observe that the whole exchange — confession and reproach together — shows conscience fully awake and the brothers turned, under affliction, to face their old crime. The verse thus records Reuben’s rebuke: his unheeded warning recalled, and the blood of Joseph required — words spoken, as the next verse reveals, in Joseph’s very hearing.
23:
On Genesis 42:23 — “And they knew not that Joseph understood them; for he spake unto them by an interpreter” — the witnesses mark the hidden hearer.

The Reformed Gill: they spoke to one another in Hebrew, and he being taken for an Egyptian, they never imagined he understood. The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: either the languages differed greatly, or Joseph, to prevent suspicion, affected ignorance of both, using an interpreter.

The verse reveals the secret. The brothers, speaking Hebrew, did not know Joseph understood every word. He overhears their confession.

So the witnesses see the poignant irony that Joseph heard, unsuspected, the whole of his brothers’ penitent confession. The witnesses note their ignorance: “they knew not that Joseph understood them” — speaking among themselves in their native Hebrew, and taking the governor for a native Egyptian, they never dreamed that he could follow their words; they spoke freely, as before a stranger. They mark the device that maintained the disguise: “for he spake unto them by an interpreter” — Joseph had conducted the whole interview through a translator, as though he knew no Hebrew, the better to conceal himself. They observe Clarke’s point that either the Hebrew and Egyptian tongues differed greatly by this time, or Joseph deliberately affected ignorance of their language to prevent any suspicion of who he was. They note the deep pathos of the moment: the very brother of whom they spoke, lamenting their cruelty to him and dreading the requital of his blood, stood before them and heard every word of their remorse — the confession he had longed for, drawn out at last. They observe that this hidden understanding is what moves him, in the next verse, to turn aside and weep; he cannot hear their grief unmoved. They note that the testing has achieved its end: their hearts are opened, and Joseph, listening, knows it. The verse thus discloses the secret: Joseph understood all they said, though they thought him a stranger — their confession reaching the very ears it concerned.

So the witnesses see the poignant irony that Joseph heard, unsuspected, the whole of his brothers’ penitent confession. They note their ignorance: “they knew not that Joseph understood them” — speaking among themselves in their native Hebrew, and taking the governor for a native Egyptian, they never dreamed he could follow their words. They mark the device that maintained the disguise: “for he spake unto them by an interpreter” — Joseph had conducted the whole interview through a translator, as though he knew no Hebrew. They observe Clarke’s point that either the tongues differed greatly by then, or Joseph deliberately affected ignorance to prevent suspicion. They note the deep pathos: the very brother of whom they spoke, lamenting their cruelty and dreading his blood, stood before them and heard every word of their remorse — the confession he had longed for, which moves him, in the next verse, to weep.
24:
On Genesis 42:24 — “And he turned himself about from them, and wept; and returned to them again… and took from them Simeon, and bound him before their eyes” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s tears and the binding of Simeon.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott: there was no bitterness in Joseph’s heart; at their first word of regret he melted, but lest he lose Benjamin he mastered his feelings and bound Simeon, choosing him as chiefly responsible. Adam Clarke and Wesley: by tradition Simeon bound Joseph, and was his bitterest enemy, now least humbled; bound before their eyes to affect them all.

The verse shows the brother’s heart. Joseph weeps apart, then binds Simeon before them. Love breaks out, yet the testing continues.

So the witnesses see Joseph’s true heart break through, even as he maintains the severity of his testing. The witnesses note the tears: hearing his brethren confess their guilt and Reuben’s tender concern for him, Joseph “turned himself about from them, and wept” — the disguise of harshness could not hold against their remorse; Ellicott observes there was no bitterness in his heart, and at their first word of regret he melted. They mark that he wept apart, hiding his tears, then composed himself and “returned to them again” — mastering his feelings, for, as Ellicott notes, he must not yet reveal himself, lest he forfeit the chance of seeing Benjamin. They observe the act that follows: he “took from them Simeon, and bound him before their eyes” — choosing Simeon to be the hostage held in Egypt. They note the reasons the expositors give for the choice: by rabbinic tradition it was Simeon who had bound Joseph and cast him into the pit, so that his binding is a fitting retribution (Clarke); and Wesley suggests Joseph saw Simeon as his most bitter enemy among them, or now the least humbled and concerned. They observe that the binding is done “before their eyes” deliberately, to impress and affect them all, deepening the solemnity of the moment. They note the union of love and severity: tears in secret, a hostage bound in public; the heart yearns while the proof goes on. The verse thus reveals the brother’s love and continues the test: Joseph weeps apart and binds Simeon — sending the rest, in the next verse, home with corn.
25:
On Genesis 42:25 — “Then Joseph commanded to fill their sacks with corn, and to restore every man’s money into his sack, and to give them provision for the way” — the witnesses mark Joseph’s secret generosity.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke notes their “sacks” (vessels), large bags lined with leather for carrying. The Puritan Henry draws the type: as Joseph gives out supplies without money and without price, so Christ; the poorest are invited to buy — yet guilty consciences are apt to take good providences for bad omens.

The verse records Joseph’s bounty. He fills their sacks, returns their money secretly, and supplies the way. Hidden grace goes home with them.

So the witnesses see Joseph, beneath his severity, lavish secret kindness on the brothers who wronged him. The witnesses note the threefold provision he commands: their sacks are filled with the corn they came to buy; their money is restored, slipped back unseen into each man’s sack; and provision is given them for the journey home. They mark the generosity hidden within the testing: while holding Simeon and demanding Benjamin, Joseph quietly returns the very price they paid and supplies their wants — mercy at work under the mask of harshness. They observe the type the Puritan Henry draws: as Joseph gives out the supplies of life freely, restoring their money so that they have corn without cost, so Christ, the true Joseph, dispenses the bread of life “without money and without price,” inviting even the poorest to buy. They note Henry’s further, searching observation that guilty consciences are apt to misread good providences as bad omens — the returned money, meant in kindness, will fill the awakened brothers with fresh terror, as the next verses show. They observe Clarke’s practical note on the “sacks,” the leather-lined bags used for such transport. They note that Joseph’s hidden bounty foreshadows the full forgiveness to come; love is already overflowing toward the brethren. The verse thus records the secret generosity: filled sacks, returned money, provision for the way — a kindness that, the next verses show, the guilty brothers receive with dread.
26:
On Genesis 42:26 — “And they laded their asses with the corn, and departed thence” — the witnesses mark the departure with the grain.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: their asses must have numbered several scores, if not hundreds, else they could not have brought enough corn for the support of so large a family as Jacob’s.

The verse records the journey home. They load the corn on their asses and depart. They carry away grain and, unknown to them, their returned money.

So the witnesses see the nine brothers set out homeward, laden with the corn of Egypt. The witnesses note the simple action: “they laded their asses with the corn, and departed thence” — the errand for which they came is accomplished; their beasts are loaded with grain and they leave Egypt for Canaan. They mark Clarke’s practical observation on the scale of it: to feed so large a household as Jacob’s, the asses must have numbered many — several scores, if not hundreds — for the corn carried home had to suffice for a great family and its dependants through the famine. They observe what the brothers do not yet know: that hidden in their loaded sacks travels not only the corn they bought but the money they paid, secretly returned by Joseph; they depart carrying a kindness they will soon misread as a snare. They note the contrast between the heaviness of their consciences, just awakened, and the heaviness of their loaded beasts; they go home burdened in body and in spirit, having left a brother bound behind them and dreading the demand for Benjamin. They observe that the departure is the quiet pivot of the chapter, from the testing in Egypt to its aftermath in Canaan. They note that the returned money, undiscovered as yet, sets up the alarm of the next verse. The verse thus records the homeward departure: the corn loaded, the brothers gone — carrying, all unaware, the money that will fill them with fear.

So the witnesses see the nine brothers set out homeward, laden with the corn of Egypt. They note the simple action: “they laded their asses with the corn, and departed thence” — the errand for which they came is accomplished. They mark Clarke’s practical observation on the scale of it: to feed so large a household as Jacob’s, the asses must have numbered many — scores, if not hundreds — for the corn had to suffice for a great family through the famine. They observe what the brothers do not yet know: that hidden in their loaded sacks travels not only the corn they bought but the money they paid, secretly returned by Joseph; they depart carrying a kindness they will soon misread as a snare. They note the contrast between the heaviness of their consciences, just awakened, and their loaded beasts; they go home burdened in body and spirit, a brother left bound behind them.
27:
On Genesis 42:27 — “And as one of them opened his sack to give his ass provender in the inn, he espied his money; for, behold, it was in his sack’s mouth” — the witnesses mark the discovery of the money.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the place and the find. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: “the inn” (lodging-place), a halting spot for the night on the caravan route. The Reformed Gill (and tradition) names the brother Levi or Reuben. Adam Clarke: only one opened his sack on the way; the rest found theirs at home.

The verse records the first discovery. At the lodging-place one brother finds his money in his sack’s mouth. The hidden kindness comes to light as a fright.

So the witnesses see the first of the brothers stumble, on the way home, upon the money Joseph had secretly returned. The witnesses note the occasion: “as one of them opened his sack to give his ass provender in the inn” — at the lodging-place where the caravan halted for the night, one brother opened his sack to feed his beast, and there, in the sack’s mouth, lay his money. They mark the expositors’ identification of the place: “the inn” was not a hostelry in the modern sense but a lodging-place, a recognized spot to pass the night on the much-travelled route, perhaps with some shelter for the beasts. They observe the discovery: “he espied his money… it was in his sack’s mouth” — the very price he had paid, restored and lying ready to hand. They note Clarke’s observation that only this one brother opened his sack on the journey and found his money; the rest, it seems, did not discover theirs until they reached home and emptied their sacks before their father. They observe that what Joseph intended as a generous kindness comes to the awakened brothers not as a comfort but as an alarm; with consciences freshly stirred, they read the unexpected money as a sign of fresh danger. They note that the discovery deepens their dread, leading at once to the cry of the next verse. The verse thus records the find: one brother espies his money in his sack’s mouth — a kindness that strikes him, as the next verse shows, with terror.
28:
On Genesis 42:28 — “And he said unto his brethren, My money is restored… and their heart failed them, and they were afraid, saying one to another, What is this that God hath done unto us?” — the witnesses mark their terror and their sense of God.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “their heart went out” — the spasm of fear felt in the breast at sudden alarm, like our own “my heart sank.” Ellicott: more poetical in the Hebrew, “their heart went forth, and they trembled.” Wesley: knowing the Egyptians abhorred Hebrews, they feared a design. The Reformed Calvin: perceiving God angry, they deplore their wretchedness.

The verse records their dismay. The restored money fills them with fear; they cry, What has God done to us? Conscience reads the hand of God in their alarm.

So the witnesses see the brothers’ terror at the restored money and their instinctive turning to God. The witnesses note the announcement: “my money is restored… and, lo, it is even in my sack” — the brother shows the others the returned price, and the discovery spreads dismay through the company. They mark the vivid description of their fright: “their heart failed them” — or, as the expositors render the Hebrew, “their heart went out” or “went forth”; Clarke notes it is the spasm of fear felt in the breast at a sudden alarm, like our own saying that the heart sinks; Ellicott observes the more poetic original, “they trembled each to his brother,” standing in terror, looking at one another. They observe the cause of so great a fear: with consciences awakened and knowing (as Wesley notes) that the Egyptians abhorred the Hebrews and that they could expect no favour, they suspect the money a snare, laid to charge them with theft and to bring fresh trouble. They note the deeper response: “What is this that God hath done unto us?” — they do not merely fear the Egyptian governor but read the event as the act of God; Calvin observes that, perceiving God to be angry with them, they deplore their wretchedness. They note that the guilty conscience traces the hand of God in the alarming providence, though it does not yet turn to him in hope. They observe that what was kindness they read as judgment. The verse thus records their dread: terrified by the restored money, they cry, What has God done to us? — the fear they carry home, in the next verses, to their father.
29:
On Genesis 42:29 — “And they came unto Jacob their father unto the land of Canaan, and told him all that befell unto them” — the witnesses mark the report to Jacob.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the narrative: arriving home, they told their father all that had occurred. The Puritan Henry: the report of the distress they had been in — suspected, threatened, Simeon left a prisoner. The Reformed Calvin: a long repetition, not superfluous, showing how anxiously they excused leaving Simeon and pleaded for Benjamin.

The verse brings the news home. The brothers reach Jacob and recount all that befell them. The trouble of Egypt is laid before the father.

So the witnesses see the brothers reach Canaan and lay the whole story before their father. The witnesses note the safe arrival: “they came unto Jacob their father unto the land of Canaan” — the journey accomplished without the pursuit or hindrance they may have feared, they are home. They mark that they “told him all that befell unto them” — the full account of their reception in Egypt: how they were suspected and accused as spies, threatened, imprisoned, how Simeon was kept behind in bonds, and how the youngest must be brought down. They observe Calvin’s note that the repetition of the story that follows, though long, is not superfluous; it shows with what anxiety the brothers excused themselves to their father for having left Simeon a captive, and how earnestly they pleaded the necessity of taking Benjamin. They note Henry’s summary that the report rehearsed the great distress they had been in — suspected, threatened, and forced to leave a brother imprisoned until the youngest should be produced. They observe the weight this news must lay on the aged Jacob, already bereaved as he supposed of Joseph: now Simeon too is gone, and the demand presses for Benjamin. They note that the report sets the stage for the father’s anguished refusal. They observe that the brothers, in telling all, must reckon with their father’s grief and their own awakened guilt. The verse thus brings the news to Canaan: the brothers recount to Jacob all that befell them — the report they unfold, in the verses following, to his dismay.
30:
On Genesis 42:30 — “The man, who is the lord of the land, spake roughly to us, and took us for spies of the country” — the witnesses mark the beginning of the brothers’ account.

The Reformed Gill: “the man, who is the lord of the land” — not the king, but the deputy governor of Egypt, whose authority under Pharaoh reached the whole land and especially the corn. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace their recital.

The verse opens their recital. They tell how the lord of Egypt spoke roughly and charged them as spies. The hard usage is reported to their father.

So the witnesses see the brothers begin the formal recital of their ordeal to Jacob. The witnesses note how they style the one who tried them: “the man, who is the lord of the land” — not, as Gill observes, the king himself, but the governor, the deputy under Pharaoh, whose authority extended over all Egypt and above all over the corn and its sale; to the brothers he was the supreme power they had to deal with. They mark the substance of their report: “spake roughly to us, and took us for spies of the country” — they tell their father plainly of the harsh reception and the grave charge laid against them, that they had come to spy out the land. They observe that the brothers begin their account with the worst of it — the rough speech and the accusation — framing for Jacob the danger they had been in. They note the dramatic irony that runs through the whole recital: the “lord of the land” whose harshness they report to their grieving father is that father’s own lost son, of whom they still speak as dead. They observe that this verse opens the repetition Calvin noted, the careful retelling by which the brothers seek to explain to Jacob why Simeon is left behind and why Benjamin must go. They note that the report is shaped to justify the demand they must now make of their father. The verse thus opens their account: the lord of Egypt spoke roughly and charged them as spies — the hard usage they unfold further in the verses following.
31:
On Genesis 42:31 — “And we said unto him, We are true men; we are no spies” — the witnesses mark their rehearsed defence.

The Reformed Gill: they recount their reply — honest, upright men, not given to treacherous or treasonable practices, who came to Egypt with no ill design, only to buy corn for the relief of their families.

The verse repeats their plea. They tell Jacob they protested their honesty and denied the charge. The defence made in Egypt is rehearsed at home.

So the witnesses see the brothers rehearse to their father the defence they had made in Egypt. The witnesses note that they report their reply to the governor’s charge: “we said unto him, We are true men; we are no spies” — they recount how they protested their honesty and flatly denied the accusation of espionage. They mark Gill’s drawing out of the sense: they had declared themselves upright men, not given to treacherous or treasonable dealings, who had come to Egypt with no hostile design whatever, but only for the innocent and necessary purpose of buying corn to relieve their starving families. They observe that in retelling this to Jacob, the brothers are at pains to show that they conducted themselves honestly under the charge, giving their father the substance of their defence. They note that the recital is part of the careful account by which they prepare Jacob for the hard demand to come — explaining how the matter unfolded, how they answered, and how the governor responded. They observe the continued irony that the “him” to whom they made this defence, and whom they now report to their father, is Joseph himself, listening to their plea of honesty from men who had once dealt with him so dishonestly. They note that the rehearsed defence leads on to the disclosure of the family they were forced to make. The verse thus repeats their plea: they told the lord of Egypt they were true men and no spies — a defence they enlarge, in the next verse, by recounting their family.

So the witnesses see the brothers rehearse to their father the defence they had made in Egypt. They note that they report their reply to the governor’s charge: “we said unto him, We are true men; we are no spies” — they recount how they protested their honesty and flatly denied the accusation. They mark Gill’s drawing out of the sense: they had declared themselves upright men, not given to treacherous or treasonable dealings, who came with no hostile design but only to buy corn to relieve their starving families. They observe that in retelling this to Jacob, the brothers are at pains to show they conducted themselves honestly under the charge. They note the continued irony that the “him” to whom they made this defence, and whom they now report to their father, is Joseph himself, listening to their plea of honesty from men who had once dealt with him so dishonestly.
32:
On Genesis 42:32 — “We be twelve brethren, sons of our father; one is not, and the youngest is this day with our father in the land of Canaan” — the witnesses mark the family disclosed again.

The Reformed Gill: all brethren by the father’s side, by one father; they had been twelve and so were still, though they knew it not, supposing one was dead.

The verse repeats the family count. They tell of twelve brethren, one gone, the youngest at home. The account given in Egypt is laid before Jacob.

So the witnesses see the brothers recount to their father the disclosure of the family they had been forced to make in Egypt. The witnesses note the count repeated: “we be twelve brethren, sons of our father” — they tell Jacob how they had pleaded their common parentage, all the sons of one father, as proof that they were an honest family and no band of spies. They mark Gill’s observation on the deep, unintended truth in their words: they say they “be” twelve, and in fact they still were twelve, though they did not know it — supposing one of them dead, while in truth all twelve yet lived, the supposed-dead brother being the very governor who had questioned them. They observe the two they mention as absent: “one is not” — the brother they believe gone, Joseph; “and the youngest is this day with our father” — Benjamin, kept at home. They note that in repeating this to Jacob, the brothers are leading up to the heart of the matter: the governor’s demand that this youngest be brought down. They observe the painful irony that, in speaking again of the one who “is not,” they touch unknowingly on their old sin against the very man to whom they had spoken. They note that the family disclosure, first drawn from them by Joseph’s pressure, is now laid before their father as the ground of the demand he must face. The verse thus repeats the account: twelve brethren, one gone, the youngest at home — the prelude to the demand for Benjamin reported in the next verse.
33:
On Genesis 42:33 — “And the man, the lord of the country, said unto us, Hereby shall I know that ye are true men; leave one of your brethren here with me…” — the witnesses mark the terms reported to Jacob.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes their tact: while owning that the lord of Egypt had spoken hard things, they do not mention that Simeon was left in bonds, nor the harsher part of their treatment, lest Jacob be afraid to send Benjamin.

The verse reports the governor’s terms. They tell Jacob the lord of Egypt set the test: leave one, bring the youngest. They soften the harsher details.

So the witnesses see the brothers lay before their father the terms the governor had set, framing them with care. The witnesses note how they relate the condition: “the lord of the country said unto us, Hereby shall I know that ye are true men; leave one of your brethren here with me” — they report the test by which their honesty was to be proved: one brother held in Egypt, the rest sent home with corn, and the youngest to be brought down. They mark Ellicott’s discernment of their tact in the telling: while they freely admit that the lord of Egypt had spoken “hard things” to them, they carefully do not mention that Simeon was left bound in chains, nor do they recount the harsher parts of their treatment — the rough words, the prison — lest the recital so alarm their father that he would refuse outright to let Benjamin go. They observe that the brothers thus shape the report to make the demand seem as bearable as possible, softening the danger in the hope of winning their father’s consent. They note the delicacy of their position: they must persuade Jacob to part with Benjamin, on whom his heart is fixed, and so they present the governor’s terms in the gentlest light they can. They observe that even so, the demand will prove more than the old man can at first accept. The verse thus reports the terms with studied care: leave one, bring the youngest — the condition they press, in the next verse, with its promise.
34:
On Genesis 42:34 — “And bring your youngest brother unto me: then shall I know that ye are no spies… and I will deliver you Simeon, and ye shall traffick in the land” — the witnesses mark the promised reward of obedience.

The Reformed Gill: they report the governor’s full terms — bring Benjamin, and they shall be cleared of the charge, Simeon restored to them, and free trade in the land granted.

The verse completes the terms. Bring the youngest, and they are cleared, Simeon freed, and free to trade. Obedience promises restoration.

So the witnesses see the brothers report to Jacob the full and hopeful terms the governor had set, with the rewards attached to compliance. The witnesses note the central demand repeated: “bring your youngest brother unto me” — the producing of Benjamin remains the one condition on which all depends. They mark the threefold promise they relay, by which they would commend the demand to their father. First, “then shall I know that ye are no spies, but that ye are true men” — their honesty will be proved and the grave charge withdrawn. Second, “I will deliver you Simeon” — the brother held in Egypt will be restored to them, their family made whole again. Third, “ye shall traffick in the land” — they will be free to come and go and to buy corn in Egypt as they need, the way kept open for their relief through the famine. They observe that the brothers, in reporting these promises, hold out to Jacob the gains that compliance would secure: vindication, the recovery of Simeon, and continued access to the grain on which their lives depend. They note that the terms, though they require the dreaded surrender of Benjamin, are framed with real inducements, that the father might be moved to consent. They observe that the whole hangs on Jacob’s willingness to let the youngest go. The verse thus completes the terms: bring the youngest, and be cleared, Simeon freed, and free to trade — the proposal that meets, in the next verses, the father’s alarm.
35:
On Genesis 42:35 — “And it came to pass as they emptied their sacks, that, behold, every man’s bundle of money was in his sack: and when both they and their father saw the bundles of money, they were afraid” — the witnesses mark the money found by all.
