The Reformed Calvin: it appears how greatly they had been alarmed on the journey, since each had not examined his sack after money was found in one; these things are written to show that when men are smitten with fear they have no presence of mind.

The verse records the general discovery. As they empty their sacks, all find their money and are afraid. The returned price alarms both sons and father.

So the witnesses see the returned money discovered by all the brothers at once, to the dismay of the whole household. The witnesses note the moment: “as they emptied their sacks… every man’s bundle of money was in his sack” — what one had found at the inn, all now find at home; each man’s price, bound up, lay restored in his sack. They mark Calvin’s shrewd observation drawn from this: it shows how greatly the brothers had been alarmed on the journey, for had they been composed, each would surely have examined his own sack after money was found in one; that they did not betrays a mind smitten with fear and robbed of presence of mind. He notes that such things are written to teach us how fear unsettles the judgment. They observe the effect of the general discovery: “when both they and their father saw the bundles of money, they were afraid” — the alarm that had seized the brothers on the road now spreads to Jacob as well; the unexpected money, far from being welcomed as a kindness, fills the whole house with dread, suspecting some snare or fresh danger. They note that the guilty consciences of the sons and the heavy heart of the father alike read the returned silver as a threat. They observe that this dismay forms the immediate prelude to Jacob’s bitter complaint. The verse thus records the general discovery: all find their money and are afraid — deepening the gloom in which, in the next verse, Jacob cries that all things are against him.
36:
On Genesis 42:36 — “And Jacob their father said unto them, Me have ye bereaved of my children: Joseph is not, and Simeon is not, and ye will take Benjamin away: all these things are against me” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s lament.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “all these things are against me” (literally, are upon me) — they lie on him as heavy burdens hastening his death; well rendered by the Vulgate, all these evils fall back upon me. Ellicott: burdens which he has to bear. The Reformed Calvin: he is angry as if, two sons taken, they hastened to destroy the third; all these evils falling on himself alone.

The verse gives Jacob’s complaint. Bereaved of Joseph and Simeon, and dreading to lose Benjamin, he cries that all is against him. The father’s grief misreads the providence.

So the witnesses see the aged Jacob break into a lament that, for all its sorrow, misjudges the hand of God. The witnesses note the bitterness of his charge: “me have ye bereaved of my children” — he lays the loss at his sons’ door, as if (Gill suggests he half-suspected) they had a hand in the fate of Joseph and now of Simeon. They mark the tally of his griefs: “Joseph is not, and Simeon is not, and ye will take Benjamin away” — one son believed dead, another held in Egypt, and now the demand for the youngest, the last of Rachel’s sons. They observe his despairing conclusion: “all these things are against me” — which, as Clarke and Ellicott note, is literally “all these things are upon me,” pressing on him as heavy burdens that hasten him toward the grave; Calvin observes that he speaks as though all these evils fell on himself alone. They note the poignant irony the chapter holds: Jacob cries that all is against him at the very moment when all is secretly working for him — Joseph alive and exalted, Simeon safe, and the whole family about to be preserved and reunited; the father reads as ruin what is in truth deliverance. They observe that this is the misjudgment of grief, which cannot see the providence at work; the believer’s “all things against me” is contradicted by the truth that all things work together for good. They note the depth of the old man’s sorrow, pressed down by loss. The verse thus records Jacob’s lament: bereaved and fearful, he cries that all is against him — a despair Reuben tries, in the next verse, to relieve.
37:
On Genesis 42:37 — “And Reuben spake unto his father, saying, Slay my two sons, if I bring him not to thee: deliver him into my hand, and I will bring him to thee again” — the witnesses mark Reuben’s rash pledge.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: a strange proposal — yet it shows the honesty and affection of Reuben’s heart; he felt deeply for his father’s distress. Ellicott: Reuben does not suppose Jacob would really slay his grandchildren, but offers a strong assurance that Benjamin would run no danger, staking the lives of his sons.

The verse gives Reuben’s offer. He pledges his two sons’ lives as surety for Benjamin’s safe return. The eldest’s earnest love overreaches in its zeal.

So the witnesses see Reuben, the eldest, try to win his father’s consent by a desperate pledge. The witnesses note the startling offer: “Slay my two sons, if I bring him not to thee” — Reuben stakes the lives of his own two children as surety, that if he fails to bring Benjamin safely home, they may be put to death. They mark the expositors’ reading of so strange a proposal: Clarke calls it strange indeed, yet finds in it the honesty and deep affection of Reuben’s heart, for he felt keenly his father’s distress and longed to relieve it. They observe Ellicott’s qualification that Reuben does not really suppose Jacob would ever slay his own grandchildren; rather, he offers the strongest assurance he can frame that Benjamin will be in no danger, reckoning the risk so slight that he is willing to pledge the lives of his sons upon it. They note the pledge of personal responsibility: “deliver him into my hand, and I will bring him to thee again” — Reuben takes the charge of Benjamin upon himself, promising his safe return. They observe that the offer, though earnest and well-meant, is rash and ill-judged; it proposes what no father would accept and what Jacob, unmoved, simply refuses. They note that Reuben’s love outruns his wisdom, and that it will be left to Judah, later, to win the father’s consent by a worthier pledge. They observe that the eldest’s zeal cannot move the grieving Jacob. The verse thus records Reuben’s rash surety: his two sons’ lives for Benjamin’s safe return — an offer the father, in the next verse, declines outright.
38:
On Genesis 42:38 — “And he said, My son shall not go down with you; for his brother is dead, and he is left alone: if mischief befall him… then shall ye bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave” — the witnesses mark Jacob’s grief-stricken refusal.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Benjamin is the only remaining son of Rachel, Joseph being supposed dead; the gray hairs and sorrow keep up the image of the oppressive burden. Ellicott: “to the grave” — to Sheol (Genesis 37:35); Jacob, of a timid and affectionate disposition, speaks as one heavy with sorrow. The Reformed Calvin: a lively picture of holy Jacob’s oppression; not iron-hearted, yet his patience the more deserving.

The verse gives Jacob’s refusal. He will not send Benjamin, the last of Rachel, lest sorrow bring him to the grave. The father’s love clings to the one son left.

So the witnesses see Jacob refuse outright to part with Benjamin, in words heavy with grief. The witnesses note the flat denial: “my son shall not go down with you” — a peremptory refusal; Wesley notes the distrust of his sons it implies, Jacob remembering that he never saw Joseph again after he had been with them. They mark the ground of his clinging: “for his brother is dead, and he is left alone” — Joseph, the other son of Rachel, being supposed dead, Benjamin is now the only remaining child of his beloved wife, the one treasure left him; to risk him is to risk all. They observe the dread he voices: “if mischief befall him by the way… then shall ye bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave” — the very phrase, Ellicott notes, of his old lament over Joseph, “to the grave,” to Sheol; should harm come to Benjamin, the old man’s remaining strength would fail and grief would carry him down to death. They note the image Clarke marks of the oppressive burden, kept up from the earlier “all these things are against me”; sorrow lies heavy on him. They observe Calvin’s tender judgment that here is a lively picture of holy Jacob’s oppression: he is not iron-hearted but feels his loss to the quick, yet his patience is the more to be admired for that. They note that the father’s love, fixed on the last son of Rachel, will not yield — though the famine and the captive Simeon will at length compel him. The verse thus closes the chapter on Jacob’s grief-stricken refusal: Benjamin shall not go, lest sorrow bring his gray hairs to the grave — the impasse that the deepening famine will overcome.
